input.article_text: The first community-owned wind turbine in the county started generating electricity last week, three years after residents voted to fund it through a local bond. The turbine, a 2.5 MW installation on farmland north of the town, produces enough power for approximately 1,500 homes. It cost 3.8 million to install. The project was led by a cooperative of 340 local investors who each contributed between 500 and 10,000 pounds. Returns are projected at 5-7% annually over the turbine's 25-year lifespan. But the project almost didn't happen. Planning permission was initially refused after objections from 12 residents who cited noise concerns and visual impact. A revised application, which moved the turbine 200 metres further from the nearest home and included a community benefit fund of 20,000 per year, was approved on appeal. The cooperative's chair, David Ellison, says the hardest part was not the planning battle but convincing people the investment was real. 'Nobody believed us at first. A community-owned turbine sounded like something from Scandinavia, not something that could happen here.' The local council has since received enquiries from three other parishes interested in replicating the model. Energy analysts say community ownership addresses the single biggest barrier to onshore wind: local opposition. 'When people have a financial stake in a turbine, they stop seeing it as an imposition and start seeing it as an asset,' says renewable energy consultant Priya Mehta.

A new study found that children who eat breakfast before school perform 15% better on standardised tests. The study followed 8,000 pupils over two years. Researchers from three universities collaborated on the project. Schools that provided free breakfast saw the largest improvements. The study was funded by a government health research grant.

Vienna's public housing system is the envy of urban planners worldwide, but it faces a challenge it has never confronted before: a population growing faster than it can build. The city owns or manages roughly 220,000 social housing units — home to 62% of the population. Rents are capped at rates that have not kept pace with construction costs. In the Seestadt Aspern development on the city's eastern edge, the newest generation of Viennese public housing is taking shape: 10,500 apartments around an artificial lake, with schools, shops, and a metro connection. The first residents moved in a decade ago. The development is now roughly two-thirds complete. 'We build for the next generation, not for the next election,' says the project's lead urban planner, Stefan Gruber. But critics say the model is under strain. Waiting lists have grown to 18 months. Young professionals earning just above the income threshold are excluded, pushed into a private rental market where prices have risen 35% in five years. An Austrian housing researcher, Dr. Klara Weber, argues that Vienna's system works precisely because it has never been means-tested in the way other countries' social housing is. 'When you include the middle class, you avoid the stigma. But when the middle class can't get in, the system starts to fracture.' The city government recently announced it would build 14,000 new units by 2030. Whether that meets demand depends on a migration rate no one can predict.
